images.original_text:
色日夜行五次七次工無間斷食飯四五頓專心練習至百日能長千觔之力此指少壯者言也即輓弱無力之人亦可練至五六百斤力倘年老精氣不足者肯如法操練日行二三次亦能健食延年除一切疾病真神妙也
余氣體素弱中年多病故友人竄見易的經苗說書朝夕攝當練氣不數月間果覺身體舒暢諸病全消年切入句以外謂非此書之功力歎因該教言以信來者
山左梁世昌謹識
第一式 面向東立目上視 兩腳踮平寬與肩 齊不可參差兩手 垂下時微曲掌背 朝上掌心朝下指 央仰翹朝前獸對 四十九字每數一 字指想朝上翹掌 想朝下捋如此四 十九韻四十九指四 十九宮也

persons.posture: Standing in profile facing left, barefoot with feet shoulder-width apart. Torso upright, weight evenly distributed. Right hand rests on lower abdomen, left arm hangs naturally at side. Head tilted slightly upward, eyes looking forward. Abdomen slightly protruding, waist wrapped with cloth.
persons.tactic: This posture represents the first martial stance (第一式) described in the text, emphasizing alignment, breath control, and mental focus. The hands’ positioning suggests a preparatory or meditative phase before movement, with the right hand on the dantian to stabilize energy and the left arm relaxed for readiness. The instruction to 'think of lifting the hand' implies internal visualization of qi flow or technique initiation.

images.original_text:
第二式
前式敬字畢將十指曲為拳背朝前以兩大指相身每數一字拳一忽大指一翹數四十九字即四十九紫又即四十九翹也

persons.posture: Standing upright with feet shoulder-width apart, barefoot. Hands hanging loosely at sides with palms facing inward. Torso erect, shoulders relaxed, head level. Belt tied around waist, loose garment draped to knees. Weight evenly distributed on both feet, body centered and still.
persons.tactic: This illustration depicts a meditative or preparatory stance for martial breathing or internal energy cultivation, associated with the 'Second Form' (第二式). The accompanying text instructs counting movements by curling fingers into fists while lifting the thumb, repeating this action 49 times — likely correlating to a qigong or martial breathing exercise. The posture emphasizes stillness, alignment, and internal focus.

images.original_text:
第三式
前式數字畢將大指疊在掌心捏緊為拳趨勢往下一伸肘之曲者後此而直以虎口向前每數一字拳加一緊次數四十九字四十九
第四式
前式數字畢將大指疊在掌心捏緊為拳趨勢往下一伸肘之曲者後此而直以虎口向前每數一字拳加一緊次數四十九字四十九

persons.posture: Standing in a relaxed, upright stance with feet shoulder-width apart. The body is slightly turned to the side, left arm hanging naturally at the side, right arm bent at the elbow with the fist clenched and held near the waist. The head is turned slightly forward, eyes looking ahead. Weight is evenly distributed between both feet.
persons.tactic: This posture represents the third form of a martial arts sequence, where the practitioner performs a series of movements involving tightening the fist and extending the elbow downward in a controlled motion. Each movement corresponds to counting one number, repeating the sequence forty-nine times to build strength and coordination.

images.original_text:
第四式
前式數字畢
將臂平抬拳
伸向前與肩
齊平如肘微
曲兇口朝上拳
掌相離尺許
教一字拳之緊
數四十九字

persons.posture: Standing in a neutral stance with feet parallel and shoulder-width apart, body facing forward. The person is barefoot, wearing a loincloth tied at the waist. Both arms are extended forward at shoulder height, elbows slightly bent, fists clenched and facing upward. The torso is upright, weight evenly distributed on both feet, head turned slightly to the left as if observing an opponent or focusing on technique.
persons.tactic: This posture represents the fourth martial form, where the practitioner raises both arms horizontally to shoulder level with fists clenched and palms facing up. It is a preparatory or transitional stance for striking or blocking, emphasizing alignment, tension, and readiness. The instruction to keep fists ‘a foot’s distance apart’ suggests spatial awareness and control, while ‘tight fist’ implies muscular engagement for power generation.

images.original_text:
第五式
前式已畢
即接此式
將兩臂豎
起虎口向
後手不可
貼頭拳宗
如前四十
九字
身力經奇說

persons.posture: Standing upright with feet together, barefoot. Arms raised vertically above head with palms facing forward (虎口向), elbows straight, shoulders relaxed. Torso erect, abdomen slightly drawn in, waist tied with sash. Head level, gaze forward, expression calm and focused.
persons.tactic: This is the fifth posture in a martial arts sequence, transitioning from the previous form. The movement involves raising both arms vertically to strengthen shoulder and upper back muscles while maintaining balance and core stability. The instruction '虎口向' indicates palm orientation for grip or energy channeling, and '後手不可貼頭拳宗' warns against bringing hands too close to the head, preserving proper distance for technique application. The reference to '如前四十九字' suggests this posture is part of a larger set of 49 instructional lines.

images.original_text:
第六式
前式已畢即接以兩拳下對耳一寸遠以兇口向兩肩數一字拳一緊想前兩肘尖往後用力四十九字

persons.posture: Standing barefoot with feet shoulder-width apart, knees slightly bent. Arms raised to shoulder height with fists clenched and elbows tucked inward, palms facing forward. Head upright, eyes looking straight ahead. Body weight evenly distributed between both feet, core engaged. The posture suggests a defensive or preparatory stance in martial arts, with fists positioned near the ears as described in the text.
persons.tactic: This is the sixth form (第六式) of a martial sequence, instructing the practitioner to transition from the previous form by lowering both fists to a position one cun (approx. 3 cm) away from the ears, with the knuckles facing outward. The movement emphasizes tightening the fists and mentally visualizing the elbows pulling back while applying force backward — likely for generating power or preparing for a strike or block. The mention of 'fourty-nine characters' may refer to a mnemonic or breathing rhythm associated with the movement.

images.original_text:
第七式
前式已畢將身往後一仰以腳尖離地為度趁勢時兩手分開直以肩齊肩口向上對一弓拳一思想的拳往上收胸微向前合數四十九字

persons.posture: Standing with feet shoulder-width apart, barefoot, torso upright. Arms extended horizontally to the sides at shoulder height, palms facing forward. Head slightly tilted back, eyes looking forward. Weight evenly distributed on both feet, core engaged. The posture suggests a transitional or preparatory stance in martial application.
persons.tactic: This is the seventh form (第七式) of a martial sequence, described as transitioning from the previous form by leaning backward slightly while lifting the heels off the ground. The movement involves extending the arms outward and then drawing them inward toward the chest in a controlled motion, repeated forty-nine times. It emphasizes balance, breath control, and internal energy circulation.

images.original_text:
第八式
前式既畢將曲手收回而拳向
前合對与第四式相同而不同
者手直肘不微曲拳對相近被
離五六寸遠數
一曰拳一宗太
合四十九字
易筋經音訛

persons.posture: Standing in a neutral stance with feet parallel and slightly apart, weight evenly distributed. The figure is bald, barefoot, wearing loose pants tied at the waist. Both arms are extended forward at shoulder height, elbows bent, fists clenched and facing each other in a closed, symmetrical position — palms inward, knuckles aligned. Torso upright, head turned slightly to the left, eyes focused forward. This posture suggests a defensive or preparatory martial stance, emphasizing balance and readiness.
persons.tactic: This illustration depicts the eighth movement of the 'Yi Jin Jing' (Muscle/Tendon Changing Classic), labeled as 'Front Form Completed, Bend Arms and Bring Fists Back.' The technique involves closing the fists and bringing them together in front of the body, maintaining alignment with the fourth form but differing in that the elbows remain straight rather than bent. The instruction emphasizes keeping the fists approximately five to six inches apart, indicating precision in distance for internal energy flow or combat positioning. The posture reflects a foundational martial application of control, centering, and preparation for subsequent movements.

images.original_text:
第九式
前式已畢將兩拳收回面兒之上雙指起即翻拳向前挺對鼻準頭拳背食指大節骨各鼻下二三分數一子拳一緊合四十九字

persons.posture: Standing in a stable, grounded stance with feet shoulder-width apart and toes slightly inward. The body is upright with relaxed shoulders. Both arms are bent at the elbows, raised to chest level, palms facing forward with fists clenched. The hands are positioned symmetrically, aligned with the nose — “拳向前挺對鼻準頭” — indicating a direct forward punch or block. Weight is evenly distributed across both feet, suggesting readiness for immediate movement.
persons.tactic: This posture represents the ninth form of a martial sequence, transitioning from a previous position by retracting the fists to the face area before extending them forward in alignment with the nose. It emphasizes precision, balance, and direct application — likely a foundational striking or blocking technique in internal martial arts, possibly related to Qigong or Shaolin boxing forms.

images.original_text:
第十式
前式已畢將兩手分開亮口對兩耳如山字形每數一弓拳一緊拳想上舉時想前近合散四十九字

persons.posture: Standing barefoot with feet shoulder-width apart, knees slightly bent. Arms raised to shoulder height with elbows bent and fists clenched, palms facing forward. Torso upright, shoulders relaxed, head level, eyes looking straight ahead. Weight evenly distributed on both feet.
persons.tactic: This is a martial arts posture for internal energy cultivation or foundational stance training. The movement involves raising the fists while maintaining a stable base, aligning the body in a 'mountain' shape (山字形) as described in the text. The instruction to 'pull the fist tight with each bow' suggests a rhythmic contraction of the arms synchronized with breathing or mental focus.

images.original_text:
第十一式
前式已畢將拳
翻轉出膝下兩
旁以曲食指大
節離臍二分
遠數一字拳一
緊數四十九字
畢即呵吞氣三
口隨津送玉丹
田

persons.posture: Standing barefoot with feet shoulder-width apart, knees slightly bent. Torso upright, arms bent at elbows with hands clasped together in front of the lower abdomen, fingers interlocked. Body facing forward, weight evenly distributed on both feet. The figure appears to be performing a meditative or internal cultivation stance, focusing on breath and energy flow.
persons.tactic: This posture is part of a qigong or internal martial arts sequence, likely emphasizing abdominal breathing, energy circulation (qi), and mental focus. The instruction to 'press the hands below the navel' suggests an emphasis on cultivating internal energy (dantian) and aligning the body’s vital forces.

images.original_text:
第十二式
此尾也炁畢不
數字而已鬆開手
垂下手掌齊向
上三端與肩平端
時腳徐跟徵起
以助其力如端重
物之狀再將拳
三舉時左往下
三扎左右忌先左
後右三跌以足全
功

persons.posture: Standing in a stable, grounded stance with feet shoulder-width apart and toes slightly inward. The body is slightly forward-leaning with the abdomen protruding. Both arms are extended horizontally to the sides at shoulder height, palms facing downward. The head is turned slightly to the right, eyes looking forward. Weight is evenly distributed across both feet, knees slightly bent for stability.
persons.tactic: This posture represents a foundational martial arts stance used to cultivate internal energy (qi) and balance. The extended arms suggest an emphasis on channeling force outward while maintaining rootedness. The text describes the movement as part of a sequence involving lifting the hands upward, then lowering them while stepping back, indicating a coordinated breathing and motion technique for internal cultivation or combat readiness.

images.original_text: 兩手托天理三焦

persons.posture: Standing upright with feet shoulder-width apart, knees slightly bent. Arms are raised straight above the head, palms facing upward, fingers interlocked or touching. Torso is erect, shoulders relaxed, chest open. Weight is evenly distributed on both feet, center of gravity aligned vertically. The figure appears to be performing a slow, meditative movement focused on stretching and energy circulation.
persons.tactic: This posture is part of a traditional Chinese qigong or martial arts exercise known as 'Two Hands Hold Up the Sky' (两手托天), designed to regulate the Triple Burner (三焦) meridian system, promoting Qi flow and internal balance. It is often used in Daoist health practices and internal martial arts for cultivating energy and strengthening the core.

images.original_text: 左右開弓似射雕

persons.posture: The figure is in a low, wide squatting stance with knees bent and feet flat on the ground. The left arm extends forward with the index finger pointing, while the right arm is drawn back toward the shoulder, elbow bent and hand near the ear as if drawing a bow. The body is slightly twisted, weight distributed evenly between both legs, and the torso is upright with head facing forward. This posture mimics the action of drawing a bow and aiming an arrow.
persons.tactic: This illustration depicts the martial arts form known as 'Left and Right Bow Draw Like Shooting a Crane' (左右開弓似射雕), which simulates archery movements to develop strength, balance, and coordination. The technique emphasizes proper stance, arm extension, and core stability for combat applications involving ranged or close-quarters striking.

images.original_text: 調脾理胃須單舉

persons.posture: Standing barefoot with left arm raised high above head, right arm bent at elbow and held near waist. Body slightly twisted to the left, weight shifted onto left leg, right leg slightly bent and supporting body. Torso upright, abdomen exposed, facial expression calm and focused.
persons.tactic: This is a qigong or martial health exercise for regulating spleen and stomach functions. The raised arm symbolizes lifting qi upward, while the lowered arm grounds energy downward — balancing internal organs through coordinated movement.

images.original_text: 五勞七傷望後瞧

persons.posture: Standing barefoot, facing slightly left with back turned to viewer; arms relaxed at sides with palms facing forward and slightly downward; head turned backward over right shoulder, eyes looking behind; body upright, weight evenly distributed on both feet.
persons.tactic: This posture is a traditional martial arts or qigong exercise for addressing 'five劳seven伤' (five labor injuries, seven trauma conditions). The movement involves turning the head backward to stretch neck muscles and improve spinal alignment, often used in internal martial arts for healing and energy circulation.

images.original_text: 接頭擺尾去心火

persons.posture: Squatting stance with knees bent and feet flat on ground, torso slightly forward, hands resting on thighs. Head tilted downward, eyes looking down, body weight centered over hips. Appears to be in a preparatory or grounding position for martial movement.
persons.tactic: The posture suggests a foundational martial arts stance used for balance and readiness, possibly part of a qigong or internal martial art form aimed at regulating energy (qi) and dispelling 'heart fire' (a metaphor for anger or overheating). The phrase '接頭擺尾' implies redirecting force or movement from head to tail, indicating a circular or flowing technique.

persons.posture: Standing behind Person One, slightly faded or ghosted, suggesting a shadow or secondary figure. Arms appear to be extended forward or in a guiding position, body upright but less defined. Likely represents an instructor, spirit guide, or symbolic presence rather than active combatant.
persons.tactic: This figure may symbolize guidance, internal alignment, or the transmission of martial knowledge. Its presence reinforces the instructional nature of the illustration, emphasizing the importance of mentorship or inner awareness in martial practice.

images.original_text: 背後七顛百病消

persons.posture: Standing upright with feet together, barefoot, hands hanging loosely at sides, body facing forward, slight smile on face, head slightly tilted upward. The figure wears a loincloth tied at the waist, and has three topknots on the head. Weight is evenly distributed on both feet, spine straight, shoulders relaxed.
persons.tactic: This illustration depicts a traditional Chinese health exercise or qigong posture known as 'Seven Dips Behind' (背後七顛), intended to promote circulation and eliminate illness by gently shaking or vibrating the body while standing still. The posture emphasizes grounding, relaxation, and alignment for internal energy flow.

images.original_text: 攢拳怒目增氣力

persons.posture: Crouching stance with feet wide apart, knees bent deeply. Left arm extended forward in a fist, right arm drawn back near the hip in a clenched fist. Body angled slightly forward, weight distributed low and centered. Head turned slightly to the left, eyes wide open, expression fierce.
persons.tactic: This depicts the martial arts form 'Zuan Quan' (Fist Clenching Fist), emphasizing explosive power generation through rooted stance and focused facial expression. The posture channels energy upward from the legs through the core to the fists, enhancing internal strength and combat readiness.

images.original_text: 兩手攀足固腎腰

persons.posture: The figure is in a forward-bending posture, with hands reaching down toward the feet while knees are slightly bent. The body is arched backward at the waist, head tilted upward, and weight is evenly distributed on both feet. The arms are extended downward, palms facing inward or toward the feet, suggesting a stretching or grounding motion.
persons.tactic: This posture represents a traditional qigong or martial arts exercise aimed at strengthening the kidneys and lower back by stretching the spine and hamstrings. It is often used for internal cultivation and physical conditioning in Chinese martial traditions.
